story:
“You’ve reached Bill’s cell, leave a message.”

Jonathan calls his father again and again. At first he’s casual—“Yo, Dad. It’s me.” Then impatient. Then worried. Bill isn’t picking up. No one has heard from him, not even Julia. Jonathan wonders if this is another of his father’s disappearing acts. He pleads for advice.

The voicemail prompt keeps interrupting his longer messages—“If you’re satisfied with your message, press 6”—as if the system, like his father, won’t let him finish. He calls Uncle Jack to check the house: locked up, car gone. Where’s the dog? He calls hospitals within fifty miles. Nothing. He files a report; the police ask if Bill has gone missing before, if he’s ever behaved strangely. Jonathan wants to tell them yes. He gives a photo. He mentions the place up at the lake. They say it’s only been a week.

He cycles through explanations: maybe Bill’s been murdered, drowned, kidnapped—or he’s run off with a new fling. He keeps leaving messages because he needs help. He needs advice. His marriage is in trouble. Eileen left a note saying she needed to get away. No return date. No promises. His job is shaky. He’s scared. He remembers when Mom left and how hard it was on his dad. He is desperate not to be alone.

Still no answer. The inbox never fills. Someone must be listening and deleting, or maybe the phone is hacked. He tells his father that what’s happening now is a metaphor for everything between them. All the times he tried to reach out and Bill wasn’t there, or said nothing, or left the house. He accuses him of being incapable of listening, of never being there. He swears. He rages. He pushes and pushes, until the anger boils over: “Let the record show that you’re the worst… I hope you’re dead.” And almost at once he breaks—sorry, he didn’t mean it, he’s just so angry, he feels like an orphan. He talks to the empty line because talking to the real man never worked. He tells his father he misses him, that he needs him, that everything has gone bonkers.

The automated voice interrupts again, lopping off his thoughts. He starts to feel crazy—“not angry, but mad, crazy mad.” He imagines his father lying in a ditch. He imagines him off with someone new. He tells himself to stop, then can’t. He keeps calling because he can’t stop needing.

Then Jonathan’s own phone buzzes with a new voicemail notification. He stares at it for a beat before pressing play. “Hi, this is Jonathan. Leave your name, number, and a short message.” His father finally calls. Calm. Breezy. “Jon, it’s Dad. Sorry I haven’t been in touch. Phone was turned off. I was with a new friend in Aruba. Her name’s Charlotte. Sorry to hear about Eileen. Give me a call.”

The loop clicks back—“You’ve reached Bill’s cell, leave a message”—and everything we’ve heard lands differently. The son’s week of panic, the police report, the accusations, the confession of hurt: all of it meets a casual apology and a vacation with a new friend. The silence, the interrupted messages, the empty inbox—the whole history of not being heard—stands intact. The son is still talking to a line that doesn’t talk back.
